facts: The largest palace complex in Rajasthan and one of India's biggest — 400 years of continuous construction (1559-1930).
Built by 22 successive Maharanas of Mewar; blends Rajput, Mughal, Medieval, European and Chinese styles.
Overlooks Lake Pichola; the Manek Chowk main courtyard hosts Rajasthan's biggest Holi festival.
Now part-museum, part-residence of the erstwhile Mewar family; open sections attract 1.5+ million visitors annually.
Centrepiece of Jaipur's UNESCO-listed walled city — planned 1729-1732 by Vidyadhar Bhattacharya for Sawai Jai Singh II.
Chandra Mahal — the seven-storey main palace — is still the private residence of the Jaipur royal family.
Houses two of the world's largest single-piece silver vessels ('Gangajali', 1902) — Guinness-record holders.
The Mubarak Mahal, Diwan-i-Aam, Diwan-i-Khas and Peacock Courtyard are open as museum galleries.
World's largest privately-inhabited residential palace by area (26 acres, 347 rooms).
Built 1929-1943 by Maharaja Umaid Singh as a famine-relief project during the 1920s drought.
Occupies 3 wings — royal residence, luxury heritage hotel (Taj), and museum.
Designed by H. V. Lanchester in the Indo-Deco style; used Chittar sandstone (hence its other name 'Chittar Mahal').
The Renaissance-inspired dome is 33 m tall — Rajasthan's tallest palace dome.
Marble palace floating on the eastern island of Lake Pichola — built 1746 by Maharana Jagat Singh II.
Occupies the entire 4-acre island; converted to a heritage hotel by Bhagwat Singh (1963).
Featured in the James Bond film 'Octopussy' (1983) and countless heritage-tourism advertisements.
One of only two 'island palaces' in India (the other is Jag Mandir, also on Lake Pichola).
Older island palace on Lake Pichola — built 1620, expanded 1652 by Maharana Jagat Singh I.
Sheltered Prince Khurram (later Shah Jahan) during his 1623 rebellion against Jahangir — the Mewar-Mughal reconciliation gift.
Shah Jahan's memories of Jag Mandir are said to have inspired the Taj Mahal's marble architecture.
Now an events venue — hosts weddings, film shoots, and the Udaipur Music Festival.
Built 1835 as a garden house for Maharani Kesar Kunwarji Ba; expanded 1925 by Sawai Man Singh II.
Home of Maharaja Sawai Man Singh II and Maharani Gayatri Devi until 1957.
India's first palace-turned-hotel — leased to the Taj Group in 1972.
Set on 47 acres of Mughal-style gardens with peacocks; one of Asia's most decorated heritage hotels.
Built 1902-1926 by Maharaja Ganga Singh in honour of his father Maharaja Lal Singh.
Designed by Sir Samuel Swinton Jacob in Indo-Saracenic style; used Bikaneri red sandstone.
Houses one of India's largest Sanskrit libraries — Sadul Museum with 21,000+ manuscripts.
Ganga Singh signed India's independence declaration on 15 August 1947 from this palace as its chairman of Rajputana.
Perched atop the Bansdara hill (944 m) — designed to observe monsoon clouds forming over Udaipur.
Built 1884 by Maharana Sajjan Singh; intended as a nine-storey astronomy observatory but never completed.
The palace grounds are now the Sajjangarh Wildlife Sanctuary — Rajasthan's smallest at 5.19 sq km.
Featured in the James Bond film 'Octopussy' (1983) as Kamal Khan's palace.
Built 1464 by Raja Rajdeo Singh Chauhan — one of Rajasthan's oldest continuously-inhabited palaces.
Sits on the Aravalli's north-eastern tail; commands the historic Delhi-Jaipur route.
Restored 1986-2000 by architects Aman Nath and Francis Wacziarg — origin of the 'Neemrana' hospitality chain (now 30+ heritage hotels).
One of Rajasthan's first heritage-hotel projects; the palace has 10 tier terraces built into the hillside.
16th-c. Samode fort built by the Nathawat Rajputs; expanded in the 18th-19th c. into a palace.
Renowned Durbar Hall and Sheesh Mahal (mirror-work) rival Amber Fort's Jai Mandir.
One of the top heritage hotels in Rajasthan; featured in 'Far Pavilions' TV series (1984).
Now the private residence of the Samode branch of the Kachhawaha Rajputs.
Rajasthan's finest garden-palace, built 1730s by Maharaja Suraj Mal of Bharatpur.
Char Bagh (Mughal quadripartite garden) design with 900+ fountains — activated once a year at the Jal Mahotsav.
Gopal Bhawan, the main palace, sits between two reservoirs — Gopal Sagar and Rup Sagar.
The palace's marble was looted from Agra by Jat forces after the 1761 Battle of Panipat.
Built 1770 by Raja Bhagat Singh of Khetri (Jhunjhunu); anchor of the Khetri Copper belt.
Swami Vivekananda stayed here as a guest of Raja Ajit Singh in 1893 before his voyage to the Chicago World's Parliament of Religions.
The name 'Vivekananda' was suggested to the swami by Ajit Singh at this palace.
The Khetri Fort library above the palace holds one of India's oldest continuously-maintained horoscopes archive.
